name: SwiftUI Preview Quality
description: Evaluates whether SwiftUI View files include quality #Preview blocks that exercise the meaningful states a view can display. Complex views with conditional rendering should have multiple named previews covering loading, error, empty, and populated states.
criteria:
# Preview Quality
**Requirement:** Custom views must have #Preview.

## Positive Signals
- Every custom view file has at least one #Preview
- Complex/stateful view: multiple named #Preview blocks covering visual states
- @Previewable @State for @Binding parameters
- Realistic mock data, not empty defaults

## Negative Signals
- Custom view file with zero #Preview blocks (MAJOR — checklist requires it)
- #Preview with empty initializers when the view requires data to render meaningfully

## Exclusions
- @main app entry point files
- Non-View files (models, services, @Observable classes)
- Simple views where one preview genuinely covers all paths

## Severity
- MAJOR: Custom view with zero #Preview blocks
- MINOR: Single basic preview despite multiple visual states

name: SwiftUI State Ownership
description: Detects misuse of SwiftUI's state property wrappers (@State, @Binding, @Bindable, @Environment) where the chosen wrapper does not match the actual ownership relationship between a view and its data. Incorrect ownership causes stale renders and silently broken reactivity.
criteria:
# State Ownership
**Requirement:** Use modern SwiftUI state management (iOS 17+). @Observable replaces ObservableObject. @State owns value types AND creates @Observable instances. @Binding/@Bindable for children. @Environment for shared dependencies.

## Positive Signals
- `@State private var store = WeatherStore()` — view creates and owns an @Observable
- `@State private var showSheet = false` — local UI toggle
- `@Binding var text: String` in child that mutates parent's @State
- `@Bindable var store: SomeObservable` — received @Observable, needs $-bindings
- `@Environment(SomeService.self)` — shared @Observable from ancestor
- `.onChange(of: value) { ... }` — correct way to react to @State changes

## Negative Signals (CRITICAL — state loss / crash)
- `@ObservedObject var vm = SomeClass()` — creates object with wrong wrapper; destroyed on every re-render
- `@State var vm: SomeVM` without default and without private — received reference type freezes after first render

## Negative Signals (DEPRECATED — iOS 17+ targets)
- `@StateObject` usage — replaced by `@State` with `@Observable`
- `@ObservedObject` usage — replaced by plain `var` or `@Bindable`
- `@EnvironmentObject` usage — replaced by `@Environment(Type.self)`
- `ObservableObject` protocol conformance — replaced by `@Observable` macro
- `@Published` property wrapper — unnecessary with `@Observable`

## Negative Signals (LOGIC ERRORS)
- `didSet` or `willSet` on `@State` — property observers never fire; use `.onChange(of:)`
- ViewModel creating its own `ModelContainer` in `init()` — wrong ownership
- `@State` on non-private property without default value

## Negative Signals (STRUCTURAL)
- 5+ `@Binding` parameters in a single view (excessive drilling)

## Exclusions
- `@State private var store = MyStore()` with default — view creates instance: CORRECT
- SwiftData `@Model` with `@State` — valid
- App-level `@State` in `@main` struct or `WindowGroup`
- `@Previewable @State` in `#Preview` blocks
- Test files

## Severity
- CRITICAL: `@ObservedObject var x = SomeClass()` (state loss)
- MAJOR: `didSet` on `@State`, ViewModel creating own ModelContainer, received reference type
- MINOR: Deprecated wrappers, excessive drilling, @State not private

name: View Decomposition
description: Identifies SwiftUI views that have grown too large and would benefit from extraction into smaller, named subviews. Evaluates body line count, @State property count, nesting depth, and number of distinct UI concerns in a single view.
criteria:
# View Decomposition
**Requirement:** Views should be small, focused, and composable. Each SwiftUI view's `body` should handle a single UI concern.

## Positive Signals
- `body` < 40 lines, composed of named child views
- Each subview handles a single visual concern
- Subview structs in own files with `#Preview`
- View uses `ForEach`/`List`/`Grid` with extracted row views

## Negative Signals
- `body` exceeds 50 lines — first signal the view does too much (MINOR)
- `body` exceeds 80 lines — strong decomposition warning (MAJOR)
- `body` exceeds 120 lines — critically oversized (CRITICAL)
- > 5 `@State` properties — state accumulating (INFO)
- > 8 `@State` properties — multiple concerns (MINOR)
- > 12 `@State` properties — model extraction needed (MAJOR)
- 4+ logically independent structural containers at the same nesting level (MINOR)
- > 5 nesting levels of structural containers (MINOR)
- Duplicated fetch: `.task { ... }` and `.refreshable { ... }` contain identical async calls (MINOR)

## Exclusions
- Long file but short body — properly extracted child structs
- Form/List with many simple one-line rows
- Non-View files, model files, @main app entry points
- Trivial wrapper views under 10 lines
- #Preview blocks
- Views containing only a single TabView or NavigationStack wrapping children

## Severity
- CRITICAL: body > 120 lines
- MAJOR: body > 80 lines; > 12 @State properties
- MINOR: body > 50 lines; > 8 @State; > 5 nesting levels; 4+ top-level containers; duplicated .task/.refreshable

name: Separation of View and Business Logic
description: Checks whether SwiftUI View structs respect the boundary between presentation and business logic. Networking, data transformation, encoding/decoding, and domain computations should be extracted into separate types (view models, services, or managers) rather than inlined in views.
criteria:
# View-Logic Separation
**Requirement:** Views describe what to show. @Observable classes handle business logic. If a View file contains URLSession, URLRequest, JSONDecoder, or JSONEncoder, the logic is in the wrong place.

## Positive Signals
- View delegates work via `.task { await store.load() }` to an @Observable class
- Networking, JSON decoding, and domain logic live in @Observable classes
- View contains only: @Query, simple CRUD (context.delete, single insert), computed filters, navigation

## Negative Signals
- `URLSession`, `URLRequest`, `HTTPURLResponse` used inside a View struct
- `JSONDecoder().decode(...)` or `JSONEncoder().encode(...)` in a View
- Private function >15 lines of non-UI logic in a View (logic that does not directly produce or configure a SwiftUI view — e.g., data transformation, validation, network coordination)
- 3+ private functions manipulating domain data in a View

## Exclusions
- `.onDelete { indexSet in ... context.delete(items[i]) }` — idiomatic SwiftData
- Simple single-line operations: `context.insert(Item())`, `context.delete(item)`
- `var filtered: [Item] { items.filter { $0.isActive } }` — simple computed filter
- `await store.fetchData()` in `.task` — correct delegation, not networking in View
- @Query + conditional rendering
- `JSONDecoder` inside a `Decodable` conformance (`init(from decoder:)`) in a model file
- Non-View files
- Code in comments or string literals

## Severity
- MAJOR: URLSession/URLRequest/JSONDecoder/JSONEncoder in a View struct
- MINOR: >15 lines of non-UI logic in a View

name: Code Hygiene
description: Identifies committed development debris: commented-out code blocks, leftover debug print() statements, Xcode placeholder tokens, buttons whose only action is printing to console, and stale file headers. Does not flag error-handling prints (covered by silent-failure-patterns).
criteria:
# Code Hygiene
**Requirement:** Use os.Logger for logging (not print()). No commented-out code. No leftover debug artifacts.

## Positive Signals
- os.Logger used for diagnostic logging
- Clean code with no debug traces
- Comments explain WHY, not dead code

## Negative Signals
- `print()` statements in submitted code (MAJOR — checklist fail item)
- Commented-out code blocks (2+ consecutive lines of commented functional code)
- Print-only buttons: `Button("X") { print("tapped") }` — non-functional UI
- Xcode placeholder tokens: `/*@START_MENU_TOKEN@*/`

## Exclusions
- Explanatory comments, // MARK:, /// doc comments, // TODO:, // FIXME:
- Code inside #if DEBUG blocks (including print() in #if DEBUG)
- os.Logger usage — correct pattern
- print() in Swift Package dependency files

## Severity
- MAJOR: print() in submitted code, print-only buttons, Xcode placeholder tokens
- MINOR: Commented-out code blocks, dead imports

name: Meaningful Naming
description: Checks that identifiers (variables, functions, types, parameters) use clear, intention-revealing names following Swift API Design Guidelines. Every flagged issue must include a specific better name — if no clearly better name exists, the issue is not flagged.
criteria:
# Meaningful Naming
**Requirement:** Follow Swift API Design Guidelines. Names must communicate purpose at the call site.

## Positive Signals
- Descriptive names revealing intent: `var temperature`, `func fetchWeather()`
- Swift conventions: camelCase properties/functions, UpperCamelCase types
- Boolean names read as assertions: `isEmpty`, `hasItems`, `shouldRefresh`
- Acronyms uniformly cased: `utf8Bytes`, `apiKey`, `urlSession`

## Negative Signals

### Convention Violations (mechanically detectable)
- **Snake_case properties/functions** in Swift code: `let user_name`, `func fetch_data()`
- **ALL_CAPS constants**: `let API_KEY = "..."`, `let MAX_RETRIES = 3` — Swift uses camelCase constants
- **Acronym casing violations**: `APIKey` (leading all-caps acronym) → should be `apiKey`
- **Boolean without assertion prefix**: `var active: Bool`, `var visible: Bool` → should be `isActive`, `isVisible`
- **File-type name mismatch**: file `Weather.swift` contains `struct WeatherService` as primary type

### Clarity Violations (require context)
- **Single-letter variables spanning multiple uses**: `let r = range.rawValue` used across 3+ subsequent lines
- **Systematic abbreviation prefix**: 3+ properties sharing a short prefix: `tplName`, `tplAmount`, `tplNote`
- **Generic names in domain context**: `data`, `result`, `value`, `item` when a domain-specific name exists
- **Cryptic abbreviations**: 2-3 character names with no vowels that aren't standard
- **Magic string sentinels**: `"NO PLAYER"`, `"UNKNOWN"` used as fallback identity markers
- **Type name embedded in variable**: `dateString`, `userArray` — redundant with the declaration

## Exclusions — Do NOT Flag
- Loop indices: `i`, `j`, `k`, `idx`; coordinates: `x`, `y`, `z`
- Closure shorthand: `$0`, `$1`; single-line ForEach/map/filter closures
- Standard abbreviations: `id`, `url`, `db`, `api`, `vm`, `ui`, `err`, `req`, `res`, `dto`, `btn`, `img`, `lhs`, `rhs`, `min`, `max`
- `data`/`response` in URLSession/Codable context
- `result` in `Result<Success, Failure>` pattern matching
- CodingKeys enum cases; non-Swift files; test files
- Single-character variables used exactly once on the same line
- `n` as a count/bound variable in mathematical context

## Severity
- MINOR: All naming issues — naming is style-level, never a functional bug

name: Fatal Error & Crash Risk
description: Identifies calls to fatalError() and preconditionFailure() in production code paths. These unconditionally terminate the app in all build configurations, losing unsaved user state with no recovery possible. Suggests graceful alternatives like error propagation and fallback UI.
criteria:
# Fatal Error / Crash Risk
**Requirement:** No runtime crashes. Every crash-risk pattern has a safe Swift alternative.

## Positive Signals
- guard let / if let for optional unwrapping
- try/catch instead of try!
- as? instead of as!
- ?? default values, bounds checking before array access

## Negative Signals
- `fatalError(` — unconditional process kill
- `try!` — crashes if expression throws
- `someVar!` force unwrap — crashes on nil
- `as! SomeType` — crashes on type mismatch
- `array[0]` without bounds checking on dynamic data

## Exclusions
- assert() / assertionFailure() — stripped from release builds
- Code inside #if DEBUG or #Preview blocks
- `required init?(coder:)` with fatalError — compiler-required, unreachable in SwiftUI
- @unknown default with fatalError — Swift-recommended pattern
- Force unwrap on compile-time string literal URL: `URL(string: "https://example.com")!`
- Force unwrap after explicit nil check: `if x != nil { use(x!) }`
- try! on JSONEncoder().encode() for types fully controlled by the developer (MINOR at most)

## Severity
- MAJOR: fatalError/try!/force unwrap in @Observable or service methods on user paths; try! on network/file I/O
- MINOR: Force unwrap on near-certain values, fatalError in config loaders

name: Silent Failure Patterns
description: Detects error-handling code that suppresses or ignores failures: empty catch blocks, catch-only-print patterns, try? on critical operations without fallback, and error logging without user-facing feedback. Silent failures hide bugs, lose user data, and make debugging extremely difficult.
criteria:
# Silent Failure Patterns
**Scope:** Errors swallowed without user-visible feedback. This practice catches code that **hides** failures; `fatal-error-crash` catches code that **crashes** the app.

## Key Differentiation from `fatal-error-crash`
| Practice | App behaviour | Patterns |
|---|---|---|
| `fatal-error-crash` | App **terminates** | `try!`, `fatalError`, `as!`, force unwrap |
| `silent-failure-patterns` | App **continues silently** | empty catch, `try?` on save, log-only catch |

## Positive Signals
- `catch` block sets user-visible error state AND optionally logs
- Error propagated via `throws`
- `try?` only in safe contexts (see exclusions)

## Negative Signals — MAJOR
- **Empty catch block**: `do { try … } catch { }`
- **Catch-only-print**: `catch { print(error) }` with no UI state update
- **`try? modelContext.save()`**: silently discards persistence failures
- **`try? await` on network call** without fallback UI
- **Catch returning nil/empty**: `catch { return nil }`, `catch { return [] }` — error info lost
- **Empty function stubs**: CRUD functions compile but do nothing

## Negative Signals — MINOR
- **Catch with Logger only**: helps debugging but user sees nothing
- **`try?` in guard-let with silent return** on network calls

## Exclusions — Do NOT Flag
- `try? Task.sleep(…)` — idiomatic Swift
- `try?` with `??` fallback on same line
- `catch { fatalError(…) }` — belongs to `fatal-error-crash`
- Catch with `throw` / rethrow — error is propagated
- Catch that sets error state variables
- `(try? context.fetch(request)) ?? []` — fetch with fallback
- `try?` in `#Preview`, test files, or `#if DEBUG`
- `try? JSONDecoder().decode(…)` with `??` fallback
- Functions with `throws` signature

## Severity
- MAJOR: Empty catch, catch-only-print, try? on save, try? on network, return nil/empty, empty stubs
- MINOR: Logger-only catch, silent guard-let return

name: Commit Discipline
description: Evaluates whether commit and merge request messages communicate the intent of each change. Flags only egregious problems: empty or meaningless messages, single generic words, and large unfocused MRs with vague titles. Short but specific messages are always acceptable.
criteria:
# Commit Discipline
**Requirement:** Issue-based development with meaningful MRs and commits.

## Positive Signals
- MR touches a single feature area
- Title clearly states what was changed
- Individual commit messages describe what each commit does

## Negative Signals
- Kitchen-sink MR: 20+ files spanning 4+ directories touching model + networking + UI + assets simultaneously
- Meaningless title: empty, ".", "asdf", "wip"
- Generic-only title without subject: "fix", "update", "changes"
- Low-quality commit messages: ".", "stuff", "fix", single-word

## Exclusions
- MRs touching 1-3 files (scope is self-evident)
- README-only MRs
- Auto-generated "Resolve ..." titles from GitLab

## Severity
- MINOR: All findings

name: MR Description Quality
description: Evaluates whether merge request titles and descriptions communicate what changed, why it changed, and how it relates to tracked issues. Good MR descriptions help reviewers understand context without reading the entire diff and create a searchable record of how the codebase evolved.
criteria:
# MR Description Quality
**Requirement:** MR descriptions must communicate what changed and why.

## Positive Signals
- Title references issue and describes the feature/fix
- Description explains what and why
- Screenshots for UI changes

## Negative Signals
- Empty description on multi-file MR
- Generic titles with no specificity
- Body contains only issue reference with zero explanation
- Unmodified MR template with placeholder text and no custom content

## Exclusions
- Draft MRs (not yet ready for review)
- Descriptive title + small change (1-3 files) — minimal body acceptable
- Trivial edits (single-line, README-only)

## Severity
- MAJOR: Empty description or unmodified template on large MR (15+ files)
- MINOR: Empty description on small MR, issue-ref-only body, generic title

name: Hardcoded Secrets
description: Detects secrets, API keys, tokens, passwords, and other credentials committed directly in source code. Hardcoded secrets in version control are the most common cause of credential leaks and are nearly impossible to fully revoke once pushed.
criteria:
# Hardcoded Secrets
**Requirement:** API keys and tokens must not be committed as string literals in source code.

## Positive Signals
- Secrets loaded from .xcconfig, .plist, Keychain, or environment variables
- ProcessInfo.processInfo.environment["KEY"]

## Negative Signals
- Prefixed tokens: sk-, ghp_, glpat-, AIzaSy, Bearer eyJ
- High-entropy strings assigned to apiKey/token/secret/password variables
- URLs with embedded tokens: `?key=ABC123`
- Firebase/cloud config with project-specific API keys

## Exclusions
- Placeholder strings: "YOUR_API_KEY_HERE", "<token>", "TODO"
- Test/mock values named mock/fake/test/sample
- Public API base URLs without embedded tokens
- Bundle identifiers
- Empty strings

## Severity
- CRITICAL: Every hardcoded secret

name: Accessibility Support
description: Ensures interactive elements and meaningful images have accessibility modifiers so assistive technologies (VoiceOver, Switch Control) can identify and describe them. Checks for semantic text styles instead of hardcoded font sizes and that decorative images are hidden from screen readers.
criteria:
# Accessibility Support
**Requirement:** HIG compliance includes basic accessibility.

## Positive Signals
- .accessibilityLabel on icon-only buttons
- Semantic font styles (.font(.title)) instead of hardcoded point sizes
- .accessibilityHidden(true) on decorative images
- Button("Label", systemImage:) or Label("text", systemImage:) patterns

## Negative Signals
- Image(systemName:) with no .accessibilityLabel and not inside a labeled container
- Icon-only Button with no text and no .accessibilityLabel
- .font(.system(size: N)) with hardcoded point sizes — breaks Dynamic Type
- onTapGesture without .accessibilityAddTraits(.isButton)

## Exclusions
- Button("Text"), Label("Text", systemImage:), NavigationLink("Text"), Toggle("Text"), Text(...) — already accessible
- Image(systemName:) inside a container with a Text sibling (including VStack/HStack)
- Image(decorative:) — explicitly marked decorative
- .accessibilityLabel on a parent container that covers children

## Severity
- MINOR: All findings

name: Error & Loading State Handling
description: Ensures views that fetch remote data present all three UI states: loading (ProgressView or placeholder), success (data display), and error (informative message with retry option). Applies only to network/remote data fetching, not local SwiftData queries.
criteria:
# Error & Loading State Handling
**Requirement:** Every view that initiates a network request must show a loading indicator while waiting and a user-facing error state when the request fails.

## Scope
Any SwiftUI View that performs networking, including:
- `URLSession`/Alamofire calls
- `Data(contentsOf: url)` — synchronous URL loading
- `.task { }` or `.onAppear { }` with `await` calls to methods that perform networking
- `async let` parallel fetches
- @Observable view models whose methods contain networking

## Positive Signals
- `ProgressView`, `.redacted(reason: .placeholder)`, shimmer while loading
- `ContentUnavailableView` (iOS 17+) for error/empty states
- `.alert(isPresented:)` with human-readable error message
- Retry button after failure
- Three-state pattern: idle/loading → loaded / failed

## Negative Signals
- Missing loading state: `.task { await fetch() }` with no ProgressView/shimmer (MINOR)
- Missing error state: network fetch with no UI error feedback (MINOR)
- Missing BOTH loading AND error states (MAJOR)
- Infinite-spinner risk: ProgressView shown but no error/timeout fallback (MINOR)
- Raw error strings: `Text(error.localizedDescription)` exposing technical messages (MINOR)

## Exclusions
- Views only reading local data: @Query, @AppStorage, UserDefaults, bundled resources
- Non-view files (services, models)
- `Data(contentsOf:)` on Bundle.main URLs
- #Preview blocks and test files
- Views where only async work is Task.sleep or animation

## Severity
- MAJOR: Both loading AND error states missing
- MINOR: One of loading/error missing; infinite-spinner risk; raw error strings
